gettingStarted.title: Getting started
gettingStarted.subtitle: Welcome to the @uu-webtools/client documentation. Learn how to integrate translations and error tracking into your application.
gettingStarted.overviewDesc: @uu-webtools/client is an npm package that provides two main modules for your Next.js or React application:
gettingStarted.overviewItem1: i18n module — file-based translations with cookie-based locale switching and missing translation reporting
gettingStarted.overviewItem2: Error module — automatic capture, error reporting, and error boundaries
gettingStarted.overviewItem3: CLI tool — pull and push translations between your project and the Web Tools dashboard
gettingStarted.overviewItemLists: Lists module — create custom lists with dynamic fields, exposed through a public API
gettingStarted.overviewItem4: Full TypeScript support with server and client entry points
gettingStarted.installationDesc: Install the package with npm or yarn:
gettingStarted.authDesc: Learn how to authenticate your requests with project tokens
gettingStarted.i18nDesc: Set up translations, locale switching, and missing translation reporting
gettingStarted.errorDesc: Automatically capture and report errors from your application
gettingStarted.cliDesc: Sync translations between your local files and the Web Tools dashboard
gettingStarted.listsDesc: Create custom lists with dynamic fields and read them through the public API
gettingStarted.prerequisitesDesc: Before you begin, make sure you have:
gettingStarted.prerequisiteItem1: An active Web Tools account with a created project
gettingStarted.prerequisiteItem2: Your project token from the project settings page

authentication.title: Authentication
authentication.subtitle: Accessing the Web Tools API requires a project token.
authentication.getTokenInSettings: You can create or view a new token on the Project Settings page in the dashboard.
authentication.requireToken: The project token is sent via the X-Project-Token header. Without it — or with an invalid token — API requests fail with a 401. When using the SDK, it's enough to set the NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBTOOLS_PROJECT_TOKEN environment variable and the token is sent automatically.
authentication.error401Missing: Token is missing — the X-Project-Token header was not sent.
authentication.error401Invalid: Invalid or unrecognized token — the token doesn't match any project.

errorHandling.title: Error module
errorHandling.subtitle: Capture and report errors from your application to the Web Tools dashboard.
errorHandling.overviewDesc: The error module provides automatic error tracking for your application. It captures errors, sends them to the Web Tools API, and displays them on your project's dashboard.
errorHandling.overviewItem1: Automatic capture of unhandled errors and unhandled promise rejections
errorHandling.overviewItem4: Custom tags and extra metadata with each error report
errorHandling.installationDesc: The error module is part of the @uu-webtools/client package:
errorHandling.environmentSetupDesc: Add your project token to your environment variables. The SDK picks it up automatically — you don't need to pass it as a prop:
errorHandling.environmentSetupNote: The token prop is optional. If not provided, the SDK uses the NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBTOOLS_PROJECT_TOKEN environment variable.
errorHandling.basicSetupDesc: Wrap your application with the NextErrorProvider component in your root layout. This sets up error capture and the error boundary:
errorHandling.basicSetupAuto1: Initializes the error client with your project token
errorHandling.basicSetupAuto2: Installs global handlers for uncaught errors and unhandled promise rejections
errorHandling.basicSetupAuto3: Wraps children in an ErrorBoundary that catches errors during React rendering
errorHandling.manualReportingDesc: Use captureException() to manually report errors, for example in try/catch blocks or API error handlers:
errorHandling.errorBoundaryDesc: You can also use the ErrorBoundary component directly to wrap specific parts of your application with a custom fallback UI:
errorHandling.errorBoundaryHow: When an error occurs during React rendering inside the boundary, it's automatically reported via captureException() and the fallback UI is shown.
errorHandling.viewingErrorsDesc: All reported errors appear on your project's dashboard in Athena:
errorHandling.viewingItem1: Navigate to your project in the Athena dashboard
errorHandling.viewingItem2: The Errors section shows all captured errors with timestamps
errorHandling.viewingItem3: View error details including stack trace, tags, and metadata
errorHandling.viewingItem4: Delete individual errors or all duplicates from the action menu

i18nPage.title: i18n module
i18nPage.subtitle: File-based internationalization with cookie-based locale switching for Next.js and React.
i18nPage.overviewDesc: The i18n module provides a complete translation system for your application:
i18nPage.overviewItem2: Locale persistence via cookies on the server and client
i18nPage.overviewItem3: useTranslation hook with optional prefix support
i18nPage.overviewItem4: Automatic missing translation reporting to the Web Tools dashboard
i18nPage.installationDesc: The i18n module is part of the @uu-webtools/client package:
i18nPage.fileStructureDesc: Create a messages directory at the root of your project with one folder per locale. Each folder contains a common.json file with your translations:
i18nPage.serverSetupDesc: In your root layout (a server component), load translations via the server entry point and pass them to I18nProvider. The locale is read from a cookie:
i18nPage.useTranslationDesc: Use the useTranslation hook in client components to access translations. It returns an object with t (translate function), locale, and changeLocale:
i18nPage.useTranslationPrefix: The optional prefix parameter lets you scope translations to avoid repeating the full key path:
i18nPage.languageSwitchingDesc: The changeLocale function returned from useTranslation sets a cookie and triggers a hard reload so the server layout re-reads the locale:
i18nPage.languageSwitchingHow: changeLocale() sets the webtools-locale cookie (1-year expiry) and reloads the page. The server layout reads this cookie via LOCALE_COOKIE_NAME to determine the active locale.
i18nPage.missingTranslationsDesc: When a translation key isn't found, the module automatically:
i18nPage.missingItem1: Logs an error to the console with the missing key and locale
i18nPage.missingItem2: Sends a POST request to the Web Tools error API with the missing key details
i18nPage.missingItem3: Returns the raw key as a fallback so the UI doesn't break
i18nPage.missingTranslationsCustom: You can also provide a custom handler via the onMissingTranslation prop on I18nProvider:
i18nPage.syncWithCliDesc: Use @uu-webtools/cli to sync translations between your local files and the Web Tools dashboard. For full documentation, see the
i18nPage.automationDesc: You can automatically pull the latest translations before running your dev server or building your app by adding pre-scripts to your package.json:
i18nPage.automationHow: npm automatically runs these scripts before the corresponding command:
i18nPage.automationPredev: runs before npm run dev — always start with fresh translations
i18nPage.automationPrebuild: runs before npm run build — ensures production builds have the latest translations
i18nPage.automationMore: For more information about the CLI, see the
i18nPage.serverExportsDesc: The server entry point (@uu-webtools/client/server) provides utilities for loading messages in server components. These must not be imported in client components:

cli.title: CLI tool
cli.subtitle: Sync translations between your local project and the Web Tools dashboard from the command line.
cli.overviewDesc: The @uu-webtools/cli package provides a set of commands to manage your translations workflow:
cli.overviewItem1: Initialize a project with the correct file structure and environment variables
cli.overviewItem2: Pull translations from the Web Tools dashboard to your local messages/ directory
cli.overviewItem3: Push local translations back to the dashboard for team collaboration
cli.overviewItem4: Check the status of your project's languages and translation coverage
cli.overviewI18nLink: The CLI works with the file structure used by the
cli.installationDesc: Install the CLI globally or use it via npx:
cli.installationNpx: Or run it directly without installing:
cli.initDesc: Initialize Web Tools in your project. This is the first command you should run when setting up a new project:
cli.initItem1: Creates a messages/ directory for your translation files
cli.initItem2: Creates or updates .env.local with the NEXT_PUBLIC_WEBTOOLS_PROJECT_TOKEN variable
cli.initItem3: Automatically pulls translations if a valid token is already set
cli.pullDesc: Download translations from the Web Tools dashboard and save them to your local messages/ directory:
cli.pushDesc: Upload local translation files to the Web Tools dashboard:
cli.pushHow: Reads all JSON files from the messages/ directory and uploads them. The dashboard displays counts of created and updated keys for each language.
cli.statusDesc: Show the current status of your project's translations:
cli.statusHow: Displays the number of languages configured and the key count for each locale. Warns about languages that have no translations yet.
cli.environmentDesc: The CLI requires a project token to authenticate with the Web Tools API. Set it in an environment file:
cli.environmentLookup: The CLI looks for the token in this order:
cli.environmentTokenLink: You can generate a token in the
cli.automationDesc: Automate translation syncing by adding npm pre-scripts to your package.json. This ensures your app always starts with the latest translations:
cli.automationHow: npm automatically runs the pre-scripts before the corresponding command:
cli.automationPredev: runs before npm run dev — always start development with fresh translations
cli.automationPrebuild: runs before npm run build — ensures production builds include the latest translations
cli.automationTip: This is the recommended setup for teams. Every developer will always have up-to-date translations without having to manually run the CLI.
cli.fileStructureDesc: The CLI expects and creates the following file structure in your project:
cli.fileStructureI18nLink: For details on how these files are loaded by your application, see the

lists.title: Lists
lists.subtitle: Create custom lists with dynamic fields and access them through the public API.
lists.overviewDesc: The lists module lets you create custom data lists with dynamically defined fields. Each list has a unique slug that can be used to fetch it from the public API.
lists.overviewItem1: Create lists with custom fields (text, number, date, select, URL, ...)
lists.overviewItem2: Slug automatically generated from the list name for easy API access
lists.overviewItem3: Two API endpoints — a list of all lists and the details of a list with its items
lists.getAllListsDesc: Returns all lists assigned to the project, including field definitions. List items are not included.
lists.getBySlugDesc: Returns a specific list by its slug, including all items ordered by position.
lists.notFoundNote: If no list exists with the given slug, a 404 error is returned:
lists.responseTypesDesc: TypeScript interfaces for the API responses:
